title: HUGO Gene Nomenclature Committee

infobox.value: **HGNC** is responsible for approving unique symbols and names for human loci, including protein coding genes, RNA genes and pseudogenes, to allow unambiguous scientific communication.

is responsible for approving unique symbols and names for human loci, including protein coding genes, RNA genes and pseudogenes, to allow unambiguous scientific communication. |

(**HGNC**) is a committee of the [Human Genome Organisation](/wiki/Human_Genome_Organisation "Human Genome Organisation") (HUGO) that sets the [standards](/wiki/Standardization "Standardization") for human [gene nomenclature](/wiki/Gene_nomenclature "Gene nomenclature"). The HGNC approves a *unique* and *meaningful* name for every known human [gene](/wiki/Gene "Gene"),[[4]](#cite_note-4)[[5]](#cite_note-bruford-5) based on a query of experts. In addition to the name, which is usually 1 to 10 words long, the HGNC also assigns a symbol (a short group of characters) to every gene. As with an [SI](/wiki/International_System_of_Units "International System of Units") symbol, a gene symbol is like an abbreviation but is more than that, being a second unique name that can stand on its own just as much as substitute for the longer name. It may not necessarily "stand for" the initials of the name, although many gene symbols do reflect that origin.

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Committee for human gene name standards

HGNC

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
|  | |
| Content | |
| Description | **HGNC** is responsible for approving unique symbols and names for human loci, including protein coding genes, RNA genes and pseudogenes, to allow unambiguous scientific communication. |
| Data types captured | [Gene nomenclature](/wiki/Gene_nomenclature "Gene nomenclature") |
| [Organisms](/wiki/Organism "Organism") | Human |
| Contact | |
| [Research center](/wiki/Research_center "Research center") | [EMBL-EBI](/wiki/European_Bioinformatics_Institute "European Bioinformatics Institute"), UK; |
| Primary citation | Braschi et al. (2019)[[1]](#cite_note-pmid30304474-1) |
| Access | |

| Download URL | [Statistics & Downloads](https://www.genenames.org/download/statistics-and-files/) [Custom Downloads](https://www.genenames.org/download/custom/) [HGNC Biomart](https://biomart.genenames.org/) |
| [Web service](/wiki/Web_service "Web service") URL | [rest.genenames.org](http://rest.genenames.org) |
| Tools | |
| [Web](/wiki/Web_application "Web application") | [HGNC Comparison of Orthology Predictions](https://www.genenames.org/tools/hcop/),[[2]](#cite_note-2)[[3]](#cite_note-3) [Search](https://www.genenames.org/tools/search/) |

The **HUGO Gene Nomenclature Committee** (**HGNC**) is a committee of the [Human Genome Organisation](/wiki/Human_Genome_Organisation "Human Genome Organisation") (HUGO) that sets the [standards](/wiki/Standardization "Standardization") for human [gene nomenclature](/wiki/Gene_nomenclature "Gene nomenclature"). The HGNC approves a *unique* and *meaningful* name for every known human [gene](/wiki/Gene "Gene"),[[4]](#cite_note-4)[[5]](#cite_note-bruford-5) based on a query of experts. In addition to the name, which is usually 1 to 10 words long, the HGNC also assigns a symbol (a short group of characters) to every gene. As with an [SI](/wiki/International_System_of_Units "International System of Units") symbol, a gene symbol is like an abbreviation but is more than that, being a second unique name that can stand on its own just as much as substitute for the longer name. It may not necessarily "stand for" the initials of the name, although many gene symbols do reflect that origin.

## Purpose

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=HUGO_Gene_Nomenclature_Committee&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Purpose")]

Full gene names, and especially gene abbreviations and symbols, are often not specific to a single gene. A marked example is CAP which can refer to any of 6 different genes (*[BRD4](https://www.genenames.org/data/hgnc_data.php?hgnc_id=13575) [Archived](https://web.archive.org/web/20131027084955/http://www.genenames.org/data/hgnc_data.php?hgnc_id=13575) 2013-10-27 at the [Wayback Machine](/wiki/Wayback_Machine "Wayback Machine")*, *[CAP1](https://www.genenames.org/data/hgnc_data.php?hgnc_id=20040) [Archived](https://web.archive.org/web/20131102135041/http://www.genenames.org/data/hgnc_data.php?hgnc_id=20040) 2013-11-02 at the [Wayback Machine](/wiki/Wayback_Machine "Wayback Machine")*, *[HACD1](https://www.genenames.org/data/hgnc_data.php?hgnc_id=9639) [Archived](https://web.archive.org/web/20131007140055/http://www.genenames.org/data/hgnc_data.php?hgnc_id=9639) 2013-10-07 at the [Wayback Machine](/wiki/Wayback_Machine "Wayback Machine")*, *[LNPEP](https://www.genenames.org/data/hgnc_data.php?hgnc_id=6656) [Archived](https://web.archive.org/web/20120913231946/http://www.genenames.org/data/hgnc_data.php?hgnc_id=6656) 2012-09-13 at the [Wayback Machine](/wiki/Wayback_Machine "Wayback Machine")*, *[SERPINB6](https://www.genenames.org/data/hgnc_data.php?hgnc_id=8950) [Archived](https://web.archive.org/web/20131008024537/http://www.genenames.org/data/hgnc_data.php?hgnc_id=8950) 2013-10-08 at the [Wayback Machine](/wiki/Wayback_Machine "Wayback Machine")*, and *[SORBS1](https://www.genenames.org/data/hgnc_data.php?hgnc_id=14565) [Archived](https://web.archive.org/web/20121012033246/http://www.genenames.org/data/hgnc_data.php?hgnc_id=14565) 2012-10-12 at the [Wayback Machine](/wiki/Wayback_Machine "Wayback Machine")*).

The HGNC short gene names, or gene symbols, unlike previously used or published symbols, are specifically assigned to one gene only. This can result in less common abbreviations being selected but reduces confusion as to which gene is referred to.

## Naming guidelines

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=HUGO_Gene_Nomenclature_Committee&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Naming guidelines")]

The HGNC published its latest human gene naming guidelines in 2020.[[5]](#cite_note-bruford-5) These may be summarized as:[[6]](#cite_note-guidelines-6)

1. *gene symbols must be unique*
2. symbols should only contain [Latin letters](/wiki/Latin_letters "Latin letters") and [Arabic numerals](/wiki/Arabic_numerals "Arabic numerals")
3. symbols should not contain [punctuation](/wiki/Punctuation "Punctuation") or "G" for gene
4. symbols do not contain any reference to the [species](/wiki/Species "Species") they are encoded in, i.e. "H/h" for human

The HGNC states that "gene nomenclature should *evolve with new technology* rather than be restrictive, as sometimes occurs when historical and single gene nomenclature systems are applied."[[7]](#cite_note-7) The HGNC has also issued guides to specific locus types such as endogenous retroviral loci,[[8]](#cite_note-8) structural variants[[9]](#cite_note-9) and non-coding RNAs.[[10]](#cite_note-10)[[11]](#cite_note-11)[[12]](#cite_note-12)

## Naming procedure

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=HUGO_Gene_Nomenclature_Committee&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Naming procedure")]

When assigning new gene nomenclature the HGNC make efforts to contact authors who have published on the human gene in question by email, and their responses to the proposed nomenclature are requested. HGNC also coordinates with the related Mouse and Rat Genomic Nomenclature Committees, other database curators, and experts for given specific gene families or sets of genes.

## Revision

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=HUGO_Gene_Nomenclature_Committee&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Revision")]

The gene name revision procedure is similar to the naming procedure, but changing a standardized gene name after establishment of a consensus can create confusion, therefore the merit of this is controversial. For this reason the HGNC aims to change a gene name only if agreement for that change can be reached among a majority of researchers working on that gene.

## See also

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=HUGO_Gene_Nomenclature_Committee&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: See also")]
